topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee in my mug felt suddenly cold against my palms. I stared at the faded photograph on the table—us, grinning like idiots under a ridiculously bright summer sun. Liam was laughing, his arm slung around my shoulder, his eyes sparkling with a shared history I felt intensely present in. Now, when I finally managed to speak, the sound caught in my throat, a dry, ragged thing. "You... you don't remember that trip to the coast?" His brow furrowed, a slight confusion clouding his features, as if I were asking him to recall a dream. The silence that followed was vast, swallowing the easy camaraderie we’d always shared. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of wood on the floor echoing too loudly in the quiet cafe. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, sinking weight pulling at my chest. I didn't say anything else; I just gathered my things, the movement stiff and jerky, and walked out into the indifferent afternoon, the world suddenly seeming muted and distant.

[story 2]

I watched him talk, the easy rhythm of his voice a soundtrack to my growing unease. Every casual reference to our shared past—a silly inside joke, a specific embarrassing moment from elementary school—was met with a blank stare, a slight hesitation before he moved on to the next topic. My jaw tightened, a hard knot forming beneath my skin. I forced a smile, a brittle, stretched thing that didn't reach my eyes, and offered a small nod when he spoke. But inside, a cold emptiness was spreading, a chilling vacuum where warmth used to reside. When he finally turned to me, seeking affirmation, his expression was one of polite distance, a gentle acknowledgment that didn't hold the depth I desperately craved. I felt a strange pressure behind my eyes, a desperate urge to retreat, to shrink away from the space between us, and I simply nodded again, the gesture feeling hollow and rehearsed.

[story 3]

It was the way he spoke of our childhood adventures that truly settled in my stomach. He described building forts in the woods behind my house with such vivid detail, the texture of the moss, the way the sunlight filtered through the canopy. I listened, my gaze fixed on his face, trying to anchor myself to the memory I held so fiercely. Then, he paused, a flicker of something unreadable crossing his features, and he shifted the subject entirely. "Oh, that was fun," he said, a dismissive wave of his hand. "What were we doing later?" The air seemed to thin around me. A tremor ran through my hands, making me grip the edge of the table until my knuckles were white. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth intense. I looked down at my hands, noticing the faint tremor, and felt a profound sense of dislocation, as if a crucial piece of my own history had simply evaporated, leaving me standing on unfamiliar ground.
